data.title: Former Intel CEO asks people to pray and fast for Intel employees
data.description:
Ousted Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) chief executive Pat Gelsinger asked people on Saturday to pray and fast for the chipmaker's 100,000 employees as investors wonder about the future of the semiconductor giant.

"This week I'd invite you to join me in praying and fasting for the 100K Intel employees as they navigate this difficult period," Gelsinger wrote in a post on X. "Intel and its team is of seminal importance to the future of the industry and US."

Gelsinger stepped down in a surprise announcement earlier in the week, replaced by co-CEOs David Zinsner and Michelle Johnston Holthaus.

Gelsinger, 63, took the reins as CEO in 2021 at a company beset with challenges. He embarked on an ambitious turnaround plan for Intel (INTC), trying to restore its lead in making the smallest and fastest chips and establishing a foundry business.

But Gelsinger was reportedly forced out after Intel's (INTC) board of directors lost confidence in his plan to turn the company around.

According to the report, the conflict was heightened last week after Gelsinger met with the board to discuss how to win back market share and narrow the gap between itself and Nvidia (NVDA). He was then given the option to retire or be removed.

The American chipmaking icon announced in August that it was letting go 15% of its staff and cut its spending and headcount by $10 billion as it works to streamline its operations.

In the most recently reported quarter, the company said it lost $0.46 per share as revenue fell 6.2% year-over-year to $13.28 billion.

Intel (INTC) shares are down over 58% YTD. In comparison, the S&P 500 has risen nearly 28%, while Nvidia (NVDA) has nearly tripled.

data.title: Omnicom is said to near mega-merger with Interpublic
data.description:
Omnicom Group (NYSE:OMC) is in advanced talks to buy longtime rival Interpublic Group (NYSE:IPG) in a deal that would create the world’s biggest advertising company, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday, citing people familiar with the matter.

A transaction could be announced as early as this week, according the newspaper’s sources.

Seeking Alpha has sought comment from Omnicom (OMC) and Interpublic (IPG), and will update this story accordingly.

The all-stock agreement is likely to value Interpublic (IPG) in a range of $13 billion to $14 billion excluding debt, the Journal reported. Interpublic’s (IPG) market value was about $11 billion as of Friday.

A combined company would have net revenue of more than $20 billion a year, based on 2023 figures for each company.

Omnicom (OMC) is the world’s third-largest ad company by revenue, while Interpublic (IPG) is the fourth-biggest. A combination would overtake WPP (NYSE:WPP) as the industry’s biggest player. WPP’s net revenue last year was about $15.1 billion, the Journal reported.

The combined company also would have some of Madison Avenue’s best-known advertising agencies under the same umbrella after years of mergers and acquisitions.

Omnicom’s (OMC) agency brands include BBDO, TBWA, FleishmanHillard and ad buyer Omnicom Media Group. Its marquee clients include Disney, Amazon, AT&T and PepsiCo.

Interpublic (IPG) owns agencies such as McCann Worldgroup, Weber Shandwick, FCB and ad-buying firm Mediabrands. Its clients include L’Oréal, Johnson & Johnson and Geico.

data.title: Microsoft's plan to win the console war: Make everything an Xbox
data.description:
"This is an Xbox" declares Microsoft's (NASDAQ:MSFT) latest ad campaign as it showcases a variety of devices running Xbox games, its latest effort to declare that every screen is now an Xbox.

The tech giant has been trying to expand Xbox to multiple devices for over five years now, as it positions the Xbox brand as a platform to play games anywhere instead of just an Xbox console.

"Whether you have an Xbox console, play on PC, with Samsung Smart TVs, handhelds, mobile phones, Amazon Fire TV, or a Meta Quest headset with Game Pass Ultimate, you can play with Xbox," the company said in a statement.

"Should Microsoft expand its partnerships to include Chromecast, Roku, Apple TV, and other television operating systems, it should expand its addressable market to over 1 billion households from fewer than 300 million now," said Wedbush analyst Michael Pachter.

"I think this is highly likely to occur, and expect Microsoft to expand its subscriber base to 200 million within 10 years," said Pachter.

Can Xbox Catch Up?

Microsoft's (MSFT) latest Xbox Series S and X consoles still lag Sony (SONY) and Nintendo (OTCPK:NTDOF) in sales numbers.

The company's much hyped Xbox Game Pass Subscription service has a target of 100 million subscribers by 2030, but it last announced numbers in 2022 when the service hit 25 million users.

According to an IGN report, Game Pass had 33 million subscribers at the end of 2023, representing growth of 13%, down from 15% in 2022.

Mobile Dreams

A big part of Microsoft's (MSFT) "Xbox everywhere" strategy is a mobile Xbox store. The company has been hinting at a "next-generation" for years, with its acquisition of $68.7 billion acquisition of Activision Blizzard only adding fuel to the fire.

Activision Blizzard library consists of mobile heavy hitters like Candy Crush and Call of Duty, which could help the company draw away users from Apple (AAPL) App Store and Google's (GOOG) (GOOGL) Play Store.

But the company's efforts seem to have been stymied by the dominant app stores' policies, on pause until regulatory cases involving the two stores work their way through courts in various countries.

No More Exclusives?

Microsoft (MSFT) has also released some of its games on Sony's (SONY) PlayStation and Nintendo's (OTCPK:NTDOF) Switch consoles, a rather controversial move for diehard Xbox fans.

Console makers often release games exclusive to their platforms to entice consumers to choose their devices.

In an interview with Bloomberg, Xbox chief and self-declared gamer Phil Spencer said that he was pleased with the results. "I do not see sort of red lines in our portfolio that say 'thou must not,'" he said.

"To reach new players, we need to be creative and adaptive of new business models, new devices, new ways of access. We’re not going to grow the market with $1,000 consoles," Spencer said.

More on Microsoft

data.title: CVC Capital Partners considering takeover of CompuGroup Medical: report
Ant Group finance chief Cyril Han to take over as CEO in March
data.description:
Ant Group said Sunday that President and Chief Financial Officer Cyril Han will take over as the chief executive early next year.

China’s largest mobile payments system operator said in a statement that Han will take over from Eric Jing on March 1.

Jing will remain chair of the company.

Han joined Ant’s Alipay unit in 2014.

Ant is an affiliate of Alibaba Group Holding (BABA).

data.title: Trump says he won't try to remove Fed chair Jerome Powell
data.description:
Donald Trump won’t try to remove Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell, the president-elect said in an interview aired Sunday.

“I don’t,” the president-elect told NBC News’ “Meet the Press” when asked if he plans to replace Powell before the chair’s term ends at the end of May 2026.

Trump told NBC that he doesn’t see replacing Powell. “But, I don’t — I don’t think if I told him to, he would. But if I asked him to, he probably wouldn’t. But if I told him to, he would.”

Powell said a month ago that he will not resign, even if Trump were to ask him to, or that he can be fired by the incoming president. “Not permitted under the law,” Powell said at the time.

Trump had threatened to fire Powell during his first administration over disagreements about interest rates. President Joe Biden reappointed Powell to the post in 2022.

Meanwhile, Trump said he couldn’t guarantee that prices for consumers wouldn’t pay more for goods covered by his threatened tariff increases against some countries.

Trump dismissed reported concerns by companies that higher tariffs would force them to pass on the added costs to consumers. “They cost Americans nothing,” he said. “They made a great economy for us.”

More on the US Economy

  • U.S. consumer credit surges past expectations in October
  • Fed's Mary Daly says jobs report shows 'labor market remains in a good position'
  • November nonfarm payrolls rebound from October's storms, strike; unemployment touches 4.2%
  • Will A Solid U.S. Jobs Report Dampen Expectation For A Fed Cut This Month?
  • Initial jobless claims increase more than expected in past week

data.title: Lifeway Food CEO says Danone offer incredibly undervalues company
data.description:
Lifeway Foods (NASDAQ:LWAY) Chief Executive Julie Smolyansky said the latest takeover offer from Danone (OTCQX:DANOY) "incredibly" undervalues the make of Kefir products.

"We're at the tipping point, and we're not we're not a brand that's distressed," Smolyansky told Seeking Alpha in an interview on Thursday. "We're not bankrupt. The world is at our back. We have ample capacity. We have an ample pipeline of innovation that we just launched. We have innovation that we're still holding back. We have distribution that we're holding back. We haven't even scratched the surface."

Smolyanski's comment comes after Lifeway last month rejected an increased $27 a share offer from Danone, after the bid was raised from $25. In addition, Smolyanski faces pressure from her own family, including her mother and brother, who jointly own 29.7% of Lifeway, and want the company to engage with Danone and said the yogurt maker's offer represents a "substantial premium." Danone has a 23.3% stake in Lifeway.

"It's priceless," Smolyanski responded when asked what she thinks the value of Lifeway should be. "I can't give you a number, but what I can say is we still have a long way to go and a lot of opportunities."

Smolyanski said she's no valuation expect and pointed out deals such as tortilla-chip maker Siete Foods recently announced $1.2 billion sale to Pepsi and Coca-Cola's $4 billion purchase of Vitamin Water's parent company in 2007.

"I think we have a ways to go to figure out the agreement and run a fair process," Smolyanski said. "Whatever is going to happen is going to follow legal guidelines, ethical guidelines, financial guidelines and a fair process. Number one, it's not going to be stolen."

Smolyanksi argues that the wind is at Lifeway's back as more consumers understand the importance of the gut to health.

"Really, the gut is the number one brain," Smolyanski explained. "It is the brain of our body, and we are just starting to learn that. So I think the fact that there is more and more science connected to our food choices, why we make certain food choices, that's really driving a lot of the growth for our for us and for other companies."

Smolyanski highlighted a new 8-ounce line of lactose-free flavor fusions that are now being sold in grocery stores, mass market, convenience stores and college campuses.

"They're flying off the shelves," Smolyanski said.

data.title: Fall of Syria’s Assad regime ignites debate on oil, gold prices
data.description:
The overthrow of the Assad family’s 50-year dictatorship in Syria has investors to debate its possible effects on the price of commodities such as oil and gold.

Amid concerns that the disintegration of the regime will leave a power vacuum in the country, where a civil war has been fought for more than 13 years, there is also hope for greater stability in the region without the meddling of Russia and Iran.

“With the fall of Assad and full capitulation of Russia and Iran in Syria, the tensions in [the] Middle East are going to decrease,” Illimar Mattus, co-founder of prop firm The Trading Pit and broker Tickmill, said Sunday in a post on social-media site X. “This means that the risk premium in WTI Crude Oil is going to decline and so the price should decline.”

While oil typically cycles through a short-term phase of rising prices in the last couple of weeks of December, the potential for further gains is “limited,” Mattus said.

Russia had backed the Assad regime, but its invasion of Ukraine has become a major diversion as the war grinds into its fourth year. Iran also was a key supporter of Assad as a major sponsor of Hezbollah. Until a recent cease-fire, the Lebanese political party and paramilitary group had been fighting Israel for 13 months.

The United States had withdrawn forces from Syria, but still maintains a force of about 900 troops in Kurdish-controlled oil drilling areas in the northeast. Another garrison is in the southeast near Syria’s borders with Iraq and Jordan.

Those forces have helped to sustain crude output, and it remains to be seen whether the end of the Assad regime will mean greater oil and gas production in Syria, Iran and Russia.

Safe-Haven Gold

The geopolitical uncertainties likely will push some investors to put money into haven assets such as gold, according to one metals strategist.

“Signs of Middle Eastern escalation should put a bid back in Gold, break the recent compressed range … and is a reminder how hard it is to be short gold given still elevated headline risk,” Nicky Shiels, head of research and metals strategy at industrial and trading services group MKS PAMP, said in a post on X.

“The fall of Assad is a good thing; the rise of a radical Islamist regime in Syria is not, which should be concerning for Iran and the Middle East region,” she said. “Especially since the US/Trump’s focus is Asia/China (trade) and Russia/Ukraine (peacedeal).”

data.title: Apple, Sony have discussed collaborating on controllers for Vision Pro: report
GenAI being adopted at double the pace of PCs or internet
data.description:
OpenAI's large language model ChatGPT has taken the world by storm since its debut merely two years ago. Rivals have poured billions into building and scaling their own AI models as hundreds of millions of users adopt these tools.

According to a working paper, genAI has been adopted at a faster pace than personal computers or the internet. The technology has a 39.5% adoption rate after two years, handily beating the internet and PCs's adoption rate of 20% in the same time period.

Researchers from the St. Louis Fed, Vanderbilt University and Harvard Kennedy School found that in August 2024, 39.4% of the U.S. population age 18-64 used genAI, with 32% using it at least once during the week they were surveyed.

The paper added that 28% percent of employed respondents used genAI at work, with 24.2% using it at least weekly, and 10.6% of employees reporting daily usage at work.

ChatGPT, the first to the race, is the most commonly used genAI program, the paper said.

Nearly one in three respondents said that they used generative AI outside of work, but only 6.4% used it outside of work every day, the paper said. Men are 9% more likely to use genAI at work and 7% more likely to use it at home.

While the researchers found that genAI use is most common in management, business, and computer occupations, with usage exceeding 40%, one in five one in five "blue collar" workers and one in five workers without a college degree use generative AI regularly on the job.

The paper estimates that between 0.5% and 3.5% of all work hours in the U.S. are currently being helped by genAI, translating to an increase in labor productivity of 0.125 and 0.875 percentage points at current levels of usage.

"However, this calculation assumes that small-scale studies are externally valid and should be treated with caution," the researchers added.
